questions.question: A $4\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $6\text{ m/s}$ collides with a $2\text{ kg}$ cart at rest. They stick together. What is the total kinetic energy after the collision?

A $3\text{ kg}$ ball moving at $4\text{ m/s}$ collides elastically with a stationary $1\text{ kg}$ ball. After the collision, the $3\text{ kg}$ ball moves at $2\text{ m/s}$ and the $1\text{ kg}$ ball moves at $6\text{ m/s}$. What is the total kinetic energy after the collision?
questions.explanation: $KE = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 2^2 + \frac{1}{2} \times 1 \times 6^2 = 6 + 18 = 24\text{ J}$. In an elastic collision, $KE$ before $=$ after ($\frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 4^2 = 24\text{ J}$).

questions.question: A $2\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $5\text{ m/s}$ collides with a stationary cart of unknown mass. They stick together. The total kinetic energy after the collision is $10\text{ J}$. What is the mass of the second cart?

A $6\text{ kg}$ object moving at $5\text{ m/s}$ strikes a stationary $3\text{ kg}$ object. After the collision, the $6\text{ kg}$ object moves at $2\text{ m/s}$ and the $3\text{ kg}$ object moves at $6\text{ m/s}$. How much kinetic energy was lost?

A $4\text{ kg}$ object moving at $6\text{ m/s}$ collides elastically with a stationary $2\text{ kg}$ object. After the collision, the $4\text{ kg}$ object moves at $2\text{ m/s}$ and kinetic energy is conserved. What is the final velocity of the $2\text{ kg}$ object?

A stationary $5\text{ kg}$ object explodes into two pieces of mass $2\text{ kg}$ and $3\text{ kg}$. The $2\text{ kg}$ piece moves at $9\text{ m/s}$. What is the total kinetic energy after the explosion?
A $2\text{ kg}$ cart moving right at $6\text{ m/s}$ collides with a $4\text{ kg}$ cart moving left at $3\text{ m/s}$. They stick together. What is the total kinetic energy after the collision?
A $4\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $5\text{ m/s}$ collides with a stationary cart. They stick together. The total kinetic energy after the collision is $20\text{ J}$. What is the mass of the second cart?

A $3\text{ kg}$ object moving at $8\text{ m/s}$ overtakes a $2\text{ kg}$ object moving at $2\text{ m/s}$ in the same direction. After the collision, the $3\text{ kg}$ object moves at $4\text{ m/s}$. How much kinetic energy was lost?
A $5\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $4\text{ m/s}$ collides with a stationary $3\text{ kg}$ cart. They stick together. What is the kinetic energy after the collision and how much was lost?

A $2\text{ kg}$ cart moving at $6\text{ m/s}$ collides with a stationary cart. They stick together and move at $2\text{ m/s}$. The kinetic energy after the collision is $12\text{ J}$. What is the mass of the second cart?
questions.explanation: Momentum: $(2 \times 6) = (2 + m) \times 2$. $12 = 4 + 2m$. $m = 4\text{ kg}$. KE check: $\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 2^2 = 12\text{ J}$.

questions.question: A $10\text{ kg}$ object at rest explodes into a $4\text{ kg}$ piece and a $6\text{ kg}$ piece. The $4\text{ kg}$ piece moves left at $15\text{ m/s}$. What is the total kinetic energy after the explosion?

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered kinetic energy calculations in collisions and explosions!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review finding unknown masses from given kinetic energy values.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on calculating velocities from momentum first, then finding KE.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: KE = ½mv², momentum conservation, and how KE changes in different collision types.
